texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Integral equation characterization of the Feynman-Kac formula for a regime-switching diffusion
texts.text: Integral equation characterization of the Feynman-Kac formula for a regime-switching diffusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Adriana Ocejo ∗
texts.text: Adriana Ocejo ∗

texts.label: text
texts.orig: December 13, 2019
texts.text: December 13, 2019

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we provide an integral equation characterization of the solution to a Cauchy problem associated to the Feynman-Kac formula for a regime-switching diffusion. We give a sufficient condition to guarantee the uniqueness of solutions to the integral equation and provide an example in the context of option pricing under the OrnsteinUhlenbeck regime-switching model.
texts.text: In this paper, we provide an integral equation characterization of the solution to a Cauchy problem associated to the Feynman-Kac formula for a regime-switching diffusion. We give a sufficient condition to guarantee the uniqueness of solutions to the integral equation and provide an example in the context of option pricing under the OrnsteinUhlenbeck regime-switching model.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Key words. Cauchy problem; Feynman-Kac; regime-switching; integral equation; contraction mapping.
texts.text: Key words. Cauchy problem; Feynman-Kac; regime-switching; integral equation; contraction mapping.

texts.text: The solution to (1.1) is known to have a stochastic representation, under suitable conditions on b and σ recalled below, via the Feynman-Kac formula (see Theorem 6 in [1]):

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where we write E t,x,i [ · ] = E [ · | X t = x, α t = i ] for short, and ( X t , α t ) is is a regime-switching diffusion (see e.g. [12]) defined on a filtered probability space (Ω , F , {F t } t ≥ 0 , P ).
texts.text: where we write E t,x,i [ · ] = E [ · | X t = x, α t = i ] for short, and ( X t , α t ) is is a regime-switching diffusion (see e.g. [12]) defined on a filtered probability space (Ω , F , {F t } t ≥ 0 , P ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Conditional expectations of the form in (1.2) arise in many applications, particularly in the pricing of financial contracts (see e.g. [10] and references therein). This expectation can be approximated by numerically solving the coupled PDE in (1.1) by finite-difference methods ([3], [5], [8]), by trinomial tree methods ([9]), or by Monte Carlo simulation ([7]). In this paper, we propose an analytical representation of v in (1.2) as the fixed point of an integral equation where we exploit the contraction theorem on Banach spaces. We give a sufficient condition for this representation to hold and provide an example in the context of commodity derivatives.
texts.text: Conditional expectations of the form in (1.2) arise in many applications, particularly in the pricing of financial contracts (see e.g. [10] and references therein). This expectation can be approximated by numerically solving the coupled PDE in (1.1) by finite-difference methods ([3], [5], [8]), by trinomial tree methods ([9]), or by Monte Carlo simulation ([7]). In this paper, we propose an analytical representation of v in (1.2) as the fixed point of an integral equation where we exploit the contraction theorem on Banach spaces. We give a sufficient condition for this representation to hold and provide an example in the context of commodity derivatives.

texts.orig: Assumption 2.1. There exist a continuous function D : [0 , T ] × R ↦→ R \{ 0 } such that for each i ∈ M , the process { e -r ( i ) t D ( t, X ( i ) t ) } 0 ≤ t ≤ T is a supermartingale and the function H ( t, x, i ) := v ( t, x, i ) /D ( t, x ) belongs to S .
texts.text: Assumption 2.1. There exist a continuous function D : [0 , T ] × R ↦→ R \{ 0 } such that for each i ∈ M , the process { e -r ( i ) t D ( t, X ( i ) t ) } 0 ≤ t ≤ T is a supermartingale and the function H ( t, x, i ) := v ( t, x, i ) /D ( t, x ) belongs to S .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The function D may be understood as a type of dampening factor of the value function v that forces it to be bounded. Dampening (or discounting) v ( t, x, i ) by D ( t, x ) allows obtaining a contractive condition for the application of the fixed point theorem. Note that if ϕ is bounded then Assumption 2.1 automatically holds by choosing D ( t, x ) ≡ 1. We give a non-trivial example in the next section when ϕ is not bounded.
texts.text: The function D may be understood as a type of dampening factor of the value function v that forces it to be bounded. Dampening (or discounting) v ( t, x, i ) by D ( t, x ) allows obtaining a contractive condition for the application of the fixed point theorem. Note that if ϕ is bounded then Assumption 2.1 automatically holds by choosing D ( t, x ) ≡ 1. We give a non-trivial example in the next section when ϕ is not bounded.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3 Example with unbounded ϕ and regime-switching Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model
texts.text: 3 Example with unbounded ϕ and regime-switching Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section we provide an example in the context of derivatives pricing where the underlying spot price is S t = e X t , and the log-price process X = ( X t ) t ≥ 0 follows the regime-switching Ornstein-Uhlenbeck dynamics
texts.text: In this section we provide an example in the context of derivatives pricing where the underlying spot price is S t = e X t , and the log-price process X = ( X t ) t ≥ 0 follows the regime-switching Ornstein-Uhlenbeck dynamics

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: dX t = β ( θ ( α t ) -X t ) dt + σ ( α t ) dW t . (3.1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Both the long-run mean log price θ , and the volatility σ , depend on the regime and are related by the expression
texts.text: Both the long-run mean log price θ , and the volatility σ , depend on the regime and are related by the expression

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where we also allow the interest rate r ( i ) > 0 to depend on the regime. Evidence from futures prices in commodities such as oil, metals, agricultural products, and electricity show that prices revert towards a mean reversion level (see e.g. [2]). The model in (3.1)-(3.2) is an extension of the one-factor model described in Schwartz [11] for commodity prices, which assumes constant parameters. Here, the regimes represent the business cycle. Empirical studies showing the effect of the economic regime in commodity spot prices can be seen in [4, Ch. 22]) and [6].
texts.text: where we also allow the interest rate r ( i ) > 0 to depend on the regime. Evidence from futures prices in commodities such as oil, metals, agricultural products, and electricity show that prices revert towards a mean reversion level (see e.g. [2]). The model in (3.1)-(3.2) is an extension of the one-factor model described in Schwartz [11] for commodity prices, which assumes constant parameters. Here, the regimes represent the business cycle. Empirical studies showing the effect of the economic regime in commodity spot prices can be seen in [4, Ch. 22]) and [6].
